content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

February 20, 1997


Honorable William L. Todd, Jr.
Wingert, Grebing, Anello & Brubaker, LLP
600 West Broadway, Seventh Floor
San Diego, California 92101-3370

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-039

Dear Justice Todd:

This is in response to your request for advice concerning the conflict of interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

You are a retired Associate Justice of the Fourth District Court of Appeal. During 1996, you tried one superior court case of fewer than 30 days. You have asked if time spent acting as a discovery referee and conducting settlement conferences should be counted for purposes of determining whether you are required to file a statement of economic interests (Form 700).

DISCUSSION

In order to avoid conflicts of interest, the Act requires public officials, including judges and court commissioners, to file periodic statements disclosing their personal assets and income and to disqualify themselves from making or participating in governmental decisions which may affect their personal financial interests. (Sections 81002(c), 87100 et seq.)

Commission regulation 18724, as amended, clarifies the filing requirements for part-time or temporary court commissioners, pro tem judges, and retired judges:

"(a) Temporary or part-time court commissioners and pro


___________________________
¹Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18100-18995 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

William L. Todd, Jr.
Our File No. A-97-039
Page 2

tem or temporary judges shall file statements of economic interests
pursuant to Government Code Sections 87200 et seq., if they serve
or expect to serve 30 days or more in any calendar year. Provided,
however, that this section shall not excuse these individuals from
any filing obligations imposed by a conflict of interest code adopted
pursuant to Government Code Sections 87300 et seq.

(b) Retired judges who serve on assignment pursuant to
Article VI, Section 6 of the California Constitution shall file annual
statements of economic interests pursuant to Government Code
Sections 87200 et seq., for each calendar year in which they serve
30 days or more. Statements filed by retired judges shall cover the
period January 1 through December 31 of the calendar year in
which 30 days or more were served, and shall be filed no later than
March 1 of the following year. Such statements shall be filed with
the Fair Political Practices Commission."

This regulation governs your service as a judicial officer, whether sitting on assignment as
a judge pro tem or as a court commissioner, for purposes of determining if you have served or
may serve 30 days or more in a calendar year.² On the other hand, employment by private parties
as a discovery referee, arbitrator, mediator or the like, does not count toward the 30-day
regulatory threshold. Therefore, if you are employed by private parties to assist in resolving a
dispute, whether or not that dispute is in litigation, this employment is not counted toward the 30-
day reporting threshold. If you are employed by the court, however, that service is counted under
regulation 18724.

Please contact me at (916) 322-5660 if you have additional questions.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

Carla Wardlow

By:    Carla Wardlow
       Chief, Technical Assistance Division

²Enclosed is a copy of the Ahern Advice Letter, No. A-93-030, in which it was determined
that court referees are generally not considered judges or court commissioners under the Act
unless a particular referee has been appointed as a court commissioner under a statute authorizing
that appointment.
